speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. Interview with Dr. Paul Parks, October 23, 1995. I was about to ask you approximately how many bodies you buried-- you did bury there and how long the process-- the procedure took?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was-- we were there for about a week. Because what slowed us down was the identification process that the Army was going through. But I would say there were several hundred. I don't know what the total count was, but it was several hundred. 'Cause we buried people in mass graves, and we were putting people in in piles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they identify people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't-- you know, some of these people had numbers on their arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I assume that's the way they did it. From the numbers only. I don't know otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had this been your first encounter with-- with German victims, or Germans in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was saying about a month before this happened, we captured about five Germans in a patrol-- what was left of a patrol we had-- in a firefight with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up just after we crossed the Rhine. And one-- I-- you know, we searched the prisoners always. And this prisoner had a picture, a picture of a naked woman, nude woman, running across an area with a group of German soldiers standing up laughing as she was running. It was a picture. And she was running toward what I now would identify as a crematorium. Because that was the kind of building that they later talked about. But that's my assumption. I have no way of knowing that. Except to know that she was running across this area and somebody took a picture of her with these soldiers looking at her and laughing. And I asked them who she was, and they just laughed and said nothing. And I kept the picture. And I don't know where it is now. I thought I had it somewhere [INAUDIBLE] locked away in my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other kinds of events after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When, you know, the war was almost over, we were headed toward-- we was headed toward the Elbe River. We never really got that far, but we were headed toward it. And we were in a series of-- Germans were surrendering, others were still fighting, and we were still moving. And we captured a whole bunch of soldiers, and we put them into a camp. And we noticed that-- I noticed that there was one of these soldiers that we captured, he was an officer. But all the rest of the guys wouldn't come to or get close to him-- the regular German soldiers. So this fellow stood in isolation as the rest of these folks just moved around. And they were very careful, but they were very respectful of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said-- I told one of my guys, I said-- told my guys, you know, we better take him out of there. Let's find out who he is. So we took him out of there, and I-- and I asked him his name. And he gave me his name and rank and serial number and that's all he gave me.

speaker: interviewee
text: First he tried to say he didn't speak English. And I said, you better learn how to speak very quickly, 'cause you better say something to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was using English.

speaker: interviewer
text: English?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an interpreter with you as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have-- we never had interpreters. 'Cause we're in the field, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not coming out there. 'Cause people are dying out there, as the guy once said to me. But anyway, so finally I just asked him. He gave me his name, rank, and serial number. When I asked him the question, he gave it-- I said, you know how to speak English. And he spit in my face. And I killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the guys said, what are we gonna tell them? I said, just tell them he tried to break loose and I shot him. He tried to escape. Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh god. It was east of about-- it must've been about half a day's ride east of Munich, it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened-- I'd gone back to my outfit, so this was my outfit I'm with now. And we-- it was north of Munich because we were-- we were slanting down toward, as I said, the Elbe River. And we were coming underneath Berlin, so we weren't going through Berlin. And it was during that period. And I don't remember the little town. There was a lot of little German towns. But we captured these guys. And [LAUGHS] the colonel asked me what happened to him. And I said, hey. He said, you should have brought him back 'cause we need to get some information from him. I said, I tried, but he tried to get away. And I wasn't gonna let him get away.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you realize you killed an SS officer? And I said, so what else is new?' [LAUGHS] But anyway. So we-- at that time-- it was just-- it wasn't long after that the war ended. What were you assigned to after you left Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: After you vacated that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war ended right after you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't long after that. At most, a few weeks and it was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh. And what kind of work were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us the job of-- we were on a roadblock coming out of Berlin. And there was-- I had-- I had-- my-- my platoon was covering this roadblock. It was a 24-hour roadblock, so we got guys on and off. Six hours on and two hours off. Two hours on and six hours off. [LAUGHS] And anyway, so we-- they gave us-- they gave us a whole book of descriptions of German war criminals and-- that we were to look for. They gave us all kinds of tattoos and all kinds of things they knew about these people, and that we were trying to find them. And so we were-- and we were to strip search everybody we saw. And I said, Women and-- He said, anybody, you strip search. Just be sure they have no contraband, no war contraband. And that if you find anything, you-- you-- you log it, you put it down, you describe it, you put in these boxes and send it back to us. And that's what we did for about-- about four weeks, five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of people that you took in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Awful lot of people, yes. And you know, I asked them all the same question-- did you know about the concentration camps? And I go, why do you think about Jews? I'm telling you, almost to a person, they all saw Jews as the bad people in their country. That they are not sorry about the ones who they got rid of, that somehow or another, they shouldn't have been there in the first place. And that Jews-- they say, one day you'll-- I remember one fellow, he said, one day you'll learn that you befriend these people and you take care of them and they'll take everything you got. Because that's what they do. And he says, they will take all your resources. They will-- and next thing you know, they will be in charge of everything and you won't have anything. And I get the lecture from him. And I said, yeah. In the meantime, take all your clothes off over in the corner, and I want to identify who you are. We didn't catch-- we didn't find anybody on our list, particularly coming  out the way we did. We didn't-- we didn't catch anybody, so to speak, with the [? German war. ?] But we got a lot of contraband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What'd you do with the contraband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Put it in the boxes, sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the contraband was jewelry and all kinds of stuff. So we-- we just put in boxes and label it and the Army came and got  it. We sent it back, they took it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in doing this roadblock, were you alone? Or were there more than one of-- how many people were [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- my platoon was doing this particular roadblock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, was this a totally black platoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a totally black outfit. With a white officer. But even then, I don't think-- my officer didn't come into the field on that one. My lieutenant wasn't there. I was in charge of that roadblock.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with-- were you still with the people that liberated Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. These were not people who had been at Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. These were-- these were back to my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only one who had been in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was the only one who'd been in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your own-- how'd it affect you seeing these Germans as now your prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I had a deep, intrinsic dislike of the arrogance of the Germans we were picking up. And for the first time, I saw their arrogance. I don't know why it got projected so, but they really were looking down their nose at us. We were the inferior people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I reminded a couple of them, if you are so superior, then walk out of here. And a fellow said, what would you do if I attempted to walk out? I said, I would kill you. And he said, so, I won't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, that's your problem. But if you're superior, you should find a way to do that. 'Cause you know, you think you're better than me, than go ahead and be better than me. But I'm looking at you now and you look like everybody else I've seen. You've got the same parts everybody else has got. You've got the same things. I don't know what makes you superior. And I've seen your dead. So you die like the rest of us. And I just don't know what makes you better than anybody else. I can't seem to find it. And I said, you're not brighter than--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back just for a minute to the group of-- that was liberating Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you, as soldiers, as a group of soldiers who were in this together, what did you talk about while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't talk about much of anything. You know, we really didn't talk about it because it was such a shock to all of us. All of us knew that the other person had feelings about it, but nobody wanted to raise it. Because we-- sometimes things happen to you that you can't handle. We were a group of-- we were a group of combat soldiers. We'd seen an awful lot of people die. But always the person we were fighting could fight back. They had weapons just like we had weapons. And that's something different about it. But to see women and children-- and it's the children that bother you more than anything else. I don't know. Somehow or another, the adults didn't bother me nearly as much as seeing little kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the thing I think all of us were affected mostly by.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got back to your camp-- I assume you went back every night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody talked about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody talked about it. We tried to think of all the good things we could say to each other. You know, we-- we tried to get everybody on the upside. We laughed about-- tried to laugh about things and all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody take pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not among us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not among you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have cameras. But the-- but the guys from headquarters took pictures. They took pictures of everybody. They took pictures. ever talk to you as a group? Did your officers talk to you about what you had seen and what you were doing as a group? No. What happened was we were talked to by the people from Army headquarters who told us what was going on and what the camp was about and all this sort of thing. They talked to us. But our officers didn't talk to us. I think our officers were in the same state-- was in the same state of shock as everybody else. It was just one of those things. You know, it's like coming from a funeral. You don't talk about the funeral. You sort of feel it. And you just don't say anything. Because the look on everybody else's face is the same one that you feel. And there's no reason to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after Dachau was liberated and you served on the roadblock were you-- did the war end?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was over when we were on the roadblock. We were-- we were-- we were assigned to several roads coming out of Berlin to try to find the war criminals, and that's what we were assigned to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what'd you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us back to Paris. And we marched in a great parade in Paris down the Champs-Elysees with thousands and millions of Frenchmen cheering. And the fireworks that went off that night. Oh, it was just a beautiful affair. And then from Paris we went to Le Havre to-- they had a series of camps named after cigarettes. So we went to Camp Phillip Morris. There was Camp Lucky Strike, there was Camp Camel. But each one of these camps was a place for-- we'd come through for redeployment back to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Camp Phillip Morris, our group did. And some-- some of the guys were taken out. Some of the guys who-- who had reached an age or something that-- and didn't have-- weren't labeled priority and who'd been over a number of months, were taken out of our group and sent to another camp where they-- they were-- they were separated out. So what was left was those of us who either were labeled priority-- and I was labeled priority as a platoon sergeant who was expert, my expertise with mine deactivation, so they took me. And we went back to-- came back to the States, went to Fort Lewis, Washington for about a month to-- to take intensive training for the invasion of Japan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were taken out on boats. And we were on a ship and we joined the large armada out in the middle of the Pacific on the way to Japan for the invasion. We told-- we'd been told that they expected to have upward toward-- we were going in the first assault wave [INAUDIBLE]-- and they felt that we would end up with about 60% to 80% casualties on the beach. And they dropped the bomb, and we didn't have to go. And they took us into the-- our group went into the Philippines. And I deactivated our outfit in the Philippines 'cause I was the last, and by that time I was a first sergeant. And we deactivated the outfit there and packed all the records. And they-- and they sent them to Tokyo, or to Japan. And I stayed out there guarding them with one officer [INAUDIBLE] who-- who never said anything to me-- a white officer who never said anything to me. And I saw him-- we were there for about six weeks-- and I just watched him deteriorate. I don't know what's wrong with him, but he did. He played this card game over and over and over and over again until I left him. I said-- one day they brought the boat out. Used to bring a boat out to bring our food and our supplies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day I said, I'm getting on the boat. He said, well, you aren't ordered to leave. I said, look, the Army better find a way to take care of this, 'cause I'm not staying out here along with that crazy man over there. You guys can take care of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left. And I got back. When I hit land, I gave the man my name. He said, oh my god. We've been looking for you. And they rushed me off in a jeep to Army headquarters, to military headquarters in-- on Leyte. And the guy said, yours-- the president of the United States said the minute that we find you to send you immediately back to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had gotten to a senator and told him, I want my son home. [LAUGHTER] 'Cause she said, he's been in Europe. He's been out-- now he's out in the Pacific. This is unfair. He's not supposed to be there. You said anybody who served in Europe wouldn't have to go to the Pacific, and there he is. I want him home. And President Truman sent an order, and I came back on the fastest ship they had coming in-- [? APA, ?] the fastest ship they had coming out of Philippines. And four days later, I was in San Francisco on my way home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was civilian life like when you got home? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember coming back to Indianapolis, and I got into a cab and told the guy I wanted him to take me to 1051 [? Roche ?] Street. And he turned and looked at me and said, I don't take nobody to nigger town. And I was always armed. Even though I came out of the Army, I still had this German souvenir of mine, [INAUDIBLE]. But it was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And I pulled it out and put it up against his head and said, look, World War III can start right now, and you're gonna be the first victim. And I said, I've got 15 clips on me. Each one has nine rounds in it, and I'll take somebody out with every round before they get to me, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you have a choice. We can start this right now or you can take me to 1051 [? Roche ?] Street. He turned white and he said, I'll take you anywhere you want to go. Took me there and said, you don't owe me. I said, oh, no, no, no. I'm gonna pay you for the cab ride. Because you're not gonna say to anybody that I held a gun on you, took you here just so I could get a free cab ride. That's not what this is about. This is about letting you know that where I've come from and what I've seen and what I'm about, and you are very lucky to still be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I took my stuff out of his car, and I left him. And I went-- about three days later, I went to Purdue. And I went to Purdue, and I went-- and I went into the dean's office. And the lady said, you-- I don't think you have an appointment. I said, you tell him who's here, will you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked out the door and  said, oh, my god. You're back. And I said, yes. I said, you know, I want to tell you something. Your statement to me when I left is one of the things that kept me alive. 'Cause many is the time I was in terrible shape and in impossible situations, and I always remembered I told you I'd be back. And I'm back. And I'm going back to school here.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, why don't you go someplace else? I said, because I want to finish at the school, this institution, 'cause I have right to. I'm a citizen of this state. I have a right to go here. I have a right to finish here. And he looked at me and he said, well, you don't have to finish. And who knows whether will you will or not? I said, that's my choice. I-- You did finish there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you get a doctorate in engineering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. But I got that from Northeastern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see. You said that he was one of the reasons that kept you alive. Were there other reasons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think it was. Because you know, one of the things-- I remember I was sitting on the roadside somewhere up in Germany. And I was heating my-- my sea rations over a can of Sterno. And this guy [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE]. This guy comes up in-- in a jeep, and he's clean and he says he's from magazine. And he said, we want to take a picture of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, why do you want to take a picture of me? He said, it's just a great picture, you sitting at the roadside here with your weapon and all. So they took a picture of me. And he said, let me ask you something. He says, how are you-- how do you, as a black soldier, reconcile the fact and live with the fact that you're discriminated against, you're segregated against, you're-- you're treated badly, and yet you fight? And yet you have a long record here of fighting 'cause that's the only way you'd be here? And he said, what's that all about? And I said, only way I can explain this is that I guess I'm fighting for the right to fight when I get back home. Is that one of the things that kept  you going too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. In fact, I'm sure it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. I just kept saying, we gotta end this crazy thing. And that's the reason why since the war, I'd been involved with Dr. King. I think I've been in a jail in every state in the South. So I've been constantly fighting for people's rights.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did your liberation of Dachau impact on your activities when you came back, and your involvement of being in every jail in the South?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I think I can explain this one. Dr. King and I sat up one night and he asked me the question, why are you here, Paul?' He said, you're here, you're going with us to all these crazy places. People throwing you in jail. Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, Dr. King, I saw a camp once where millions of people had been through and died. And I said, you know something? I know what the end of bigotry looks like-- the solution to bigotry looks like from the standpoint of the bigot. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people who we're fighting to change, I-- after they had killed the little kids down in Birmingham and blown up the church, I said, that is not an unusual activity for people who are haters, who believe that they're better than the people, and who believe the solution to those other people is to destroy them. And I've seen that. And I don't want that to ever happen again. And my commitment is to try to see it. And I really am a person of peace. He says, you're learning. He said-- he talked to me all the time. He said, you've got to put behind you a lot of the things that's eating away at you. You really do. He said, you gotta go the mountaintop and think about yourself and get yourself to a point where you can-- you can be a positive force for change. And not have that feeling that somehow or another, you want to go to war. You can't kill anybody. 'Cause I was-- you know, I came very close several times. I was in-- I was in a restaurant in Charleston, South Carolina. And we were-- we were sitting in the restaurant. And this woman waitress-- there were three of us sitting there-- got up on top of the-- top of the counter and urinated on us. I came as close as I have ever come to losing it that day. But I said-- the guy grabbed me by the arm and said, let's get out of here. But we didn't get a chance to, because they arrested us for-- for being indecent because they said we looked at this women when she was urinating on us. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that some of that anger that you brought back from the liberation of Dachau is still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I have the anger. Except I can't say that. When I was in-- in Barcelona at the Olympics, I wanted to get some additional tickets to fill out my itinerary in track and field. And I got them-- I got-- the vice president of Coca-Cola's a friend of mine-- gave me the tickets. But he gave me the tickets in the German section. And I was sitting with these-- sitting in this seat, which we were pushed together and could hardly move. And I sat down beside this fellow who was a German fellow who swung around from me as far as he could and said he didn't want the likes of me touching him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I sat there looking at him. And my wife said, what did you say to that man? Or what did he say to you? I told her and she said, what did you say to him? And I just said, I didn't say anything. Said, well, he turned white as a sheet. What did you do? I said, I just looked at him. She said, I know that look that you have. I've seen it a couple of times. I said-- she said, it's like staring in the eyes of death. I  said, I don't know about this. [LAUGHS] I said, all I know is that  this man being very close to me, pitching him out there on the track, OK? And then I said to myself, no, that's not a civilized move.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember Dr. King telling me to learn how to-- to live with your anger. And I just looked at him and ignored him. I said-- he-- Dr. King said, every time you lose it to someone like that, what you do is [? dimunize ?] yourself. You've got to be able to stand taller than these kinds of things and to walk away and to keep pushing to get rid of the issues that those people are espousing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw a-- a unbelievable scene in Dachau. How do you handle people who are espousing Holocaust denial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been the-- now that's when I been-- I've spoken before groups. Because several-- Jewish Community Council and all these people have gotten me to speak before various groups. And I've spoken before a group of people who said they didn't believe it, it was all--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, look, let me tell you something. This ain't your mind. I don't have a picture except in my own mind, but I was there and I saw it. They said, are you sure? You were just dreaming. I said, no, I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going to tell you something, and I'm gonna tell all of you something. If you don't understand this and you don't listen to what people are saying, we are doomed to repeat it again. And it makes no sense. It was a group out at-- out at the Charlestown Army Veteran's Home. And I said, most of you guys in this room know exactly what the war was like. You weren't there at Dachau, or at any of the camps, so you're telling me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm gonna tell you something. Why did we fight the war in the first place? And who were we fighting? And what was that about? And think about it. It's a very short step between what you saw in the field and what happened at Dachau. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I told them once again, we know you saw the little kids blown up in England. You saw all of that. And that's because of the fact that one man and his followers believed that they were better than everybody else in the world. And you heard them talk about that they were Aryans. I don't know what an Aryan is. But I heard Germans talk about the fact that they were important, they were somebody. But one human being can't be any better than another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will never go back to Germany. I-- I have never back to Germany. I've been in Europe many, many times, but I've never gone back to Germany, and I don't ever intend to. People at the German embassy-- one day I was out picketing the German embassy about the end of the-- end of the limitations-- and they asked me to come inside. And the thing that he got to me, because when we got inside, he says, welcome to the land of Germany. And I'm in the embassy and I'm saying, no, no no, no, no, no. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, what is the problem? And I told him. He said, well, I was a little baby then, so I had nothing to do with that. I said, had nothing to do with that. I said, well, I don't buy anything made in Germany if I know it. And I don't go to Germany. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm saying that's my own private war. Because I said when I saw Dachau that I thought that the Germans should not be allowed back into the civilized world with the rest of human beings for 1,000 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Change.
